metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

coverage.verify_error: step 'review@then/verify_branch@then/verify' failed: the model hit the token cap before finishing (finish_reason=length) — its output is TRUNCATED; raise max_tokens or split the step

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit bootstrap/init step to the plan (or to `autodeploy.sh` itself) that runs `git clone --mirror <URL> $MIRROR_DIR` on first run if the directory does not exist, and name the public GitHub remote URL.
findings.finding: The autodeploy loop assumes MIRROR_DIR=/var/lib/rebar/mirror already exists as a git clone, but the plan nowhere specifies the initialization procedure (git clone URL, auth method, or first-run bootstrap command). An executing agent cannot create the mirror without knowing the public GitHub remote URL or confirming 'public = unauthenticated HTTPS'.
findings.location: Box-adaptation section, 'Adapted mechanism' step 1: 'autodeploy maintains its OWN git mirror clone at MIRROR_DIR=/var/lib/rebar/mirror (a clone of the PUBLIC GitHub mirror, read-only).'
findings.suggested_fix: In `autodeploy.sh`, add an initialization block: `if [ ! -d "$MIRROR_DIR" ]; then git clone --mirror https://github.com/<org>/<repo>.git "$MIRROR_DIR"; fi` and document the URL in the plan's box-adaptation section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] For each AC that claims 'proven live' or 'verified', add a named proving command or evidence artifact (e.g., for AC3: '`docker inspect gerrit --format {{.ID}}` recorded before and after a deploy and confirmed identical'; for AC4: 'run `./autodeploy.sh --inject-broken-image` and confirm `:prev` is running via `docker ps` and `/var/lib/rebar/deployed-sha` is unchanged').
findings.finding: Multiple acceptance criteria claim outcomes are 'proven' or 'verified' but do not name the specific proving command or evidence artifact that would demonstrate the claim on the real box — meaning an agent can sign off without producing concrete evidence. Affected ACs: (1) '≤5 min latency live-measured' (no timestamp extraction command), (3) 'gerrit container is provably untouched' (no docker-inspect command cited in AC), (4) 'proven live' rollback (no command sequence to trigger and assert), and box-adaptation AC2 'manual dry-run' (no command sequence specified).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section, ACs 1, 3, 4, and 'Additional acceptance criteria' AC2

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Split AC7 into at least three separate criteria: (i) flock + deploy log (script-level), (ii) deploy_errors metric + CloudWatch alarm wired and fire-tested, (iii) back-out (timer disable) and manual path smoke-test — each with its own named proving command.
findings.finding: Acceptance Criterion 7 bundles seven distinct verifiable outcomes — (a) flock serialisation, (b) deploy log, (c) deploy_errors metric, (d) CloudWatch alarm, (e) back-out via timer disable, (f) manual path still works, (g) live end-to-end auto-deploy — into a single criterion, violating the 'coherent single-outcome deliverable' requirement and making it untractable to declare partial completion or diagnose which sub-item failed.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section, AC7: 'flock serializes overlapping fires; deploy log + deploy_errors metric + alarm applied; back-out (disable timer) tested; manual path still works; live end-to-end auto-deploy verified'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the terminology in AC3 ('project.config') with the What section ('refs/meta/config'): either broaden AC3 to 'any file within refs/meta/config (project.config, groups, …) is detected + signalled' or explicitly state which files are in scope for detection.
findings.finding: AC3 says 'project.config changes are detected + signalled, not attempted', but the What section says the entire `refs/meta/config` REF is out of scope ('refs/meta/config is OUT of v1'). In Gerrit, `refs/meta/config` contains multiple files (project.config, groups, etc.). Using `git diff --name-only` on the working-tree path 'project.config' would miss changes to the 'groups' file or any other file within that ref. The AC's narrower term 'project.config' under-specifies what the plan's broader 'refs/meta/config' intent covers.
findings.location: What section ('refs/meta/config is OUT of v1') vs AC3 ('project.config changes are detected + signalled, not attempted')
findings.suggested_fix: Update AC3 to: 'refs/meta/config changes (project.config, groups, and any other file within that ref) are detected + signalled, never auto-applied — verified by injecting a change to each file and confirming the signal fires.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section documenting the on-box systemd-timer auto-deploy pattern (vs. GitHub→SSM), the security rationale, the mirror/rsync contract, and the back-out procedure (`systemctl disable --now rebar-autodeploy.timer`) so operators can act without reading this ticket.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a significant new architectural pattern (on-box systemd timer auto-deploy instead of GitHub-Actions→SSM, with an explicit security-rationale tradeoff) and new operational contracts (mirror at /var/lib/rebar/mirror, rsync excludes protecting .env and Docker volumes, back-out via `systemctl disable`, make config-check gate) but does not mention updating CLAUDE.md, a README, a runbook, or an ADR. An operator responding to an incident would not know this mechanism exists or how to disable it without reading the original ticket.
findings.location: ## Scope / ## Observability + back-out / ## Box-adaptation — overall plan
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `docs/adr/autodeploy.md` (or a CLAUDE.md `## Auto-deploy` section) covering: (1) why on-box timer not GH Actions→SSM, (2) mirror location and rsync exclude contract, (3) back-out and manual override, (4) `make config-check` as the CI gate, (5) backoff/rollback behavior. Reference it from Scope in this plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Make the key operational thresholds (backoff base/factor/cap, health-check timeout, poll interval) configurable via environment variables read by autodeploy.sh (e.g., sourced from `/etc/rebar/autodeploy.env`) so they can be tuned without editing the script.
findings.finding: Several operational thresholds embedded in autodeploy.sh are mentioned as fixed values (backoff base=60s, factor=2, cap=15m; health-check timeout=30s; timer poll interval=2min) with no plan to make them configurable. These are tunable operational parameters that are likely to need adjustment based on observed deploy latency and gate SLAs.
findings.location: ## Stability §4 / Acceptance Criteria (≤5 min latency) / Box-adaptation (systemd unit)
findings.suggested_fix: In autodeploy.sh, add `AUTODEPLOY_BACKOFF_BASE=${AUTODEPLOY_BACKOFF_BASE:-60}`, `AUTODEPLOY_BACKOFF_CAP=${AUTODEPLOY_BACKOFF_CAP:-900}`, `AUTODEPLOY_HEALTH_TIMEOUT=${AUTODEPLOY_HEALTH_TIMEOUT:-30}` at the top, sourced optionally from `/etc/rebar/autodeploy.env`. The systemd timer `OnActiveSec=2min` can be a unit drop-in override if needed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly instrument git-fetch / mirror-sync failures as AUTODEPLOY_ERROR entries (same marker + CloudWatch alarm path used for deploy failures), so a persistent fetch outage pages operators rather than silently stalling the loop.
findings.finding: Persistent git-fetch failures (e.g. GitHub public mirror unreachable) are not explicitly instrumented: the backoff/alarm language targets 'deploy errors', leaving a scenario where the autodeploy loop silently stalls for hours with no operator alert, while the system appears healthy (last-known-good bot still running).
findings.location: Observability + back-out section; Box-adaptation §1 (mirror clone fetch step); AC line 7 (deploy_errors metric)
findings.suggested_fix: In autodeploy.sh, wrap the `git fetch` step: if it fails, emit `AUTODEPLOY_ERROR fetch_failed <reason>` to the structured log (same format as deploy errors), increment the backoff counter (or use a separate fetch-failure counter), and let the existing alarm pick it up. Document this in the Observability section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a RETIRE/cleanup step for Docker image accumulation — e.g., `docker image prune -f` (or `--filter until=24h`) after each successful deploy, or a periodic systemd timer — so dangling build layers don't accumulate indefinitely.
findings.finding: Each `docker compose up -d --build` produces new image layers; only the `:prev` tag is explicitly managed, but dangling intermediate layers accumulate indefinitely — the plan defines no `docker image prune` or equivalent cleanup step.
findings.location: Stability §1 / Box-adaptation §Adapted mechanism step 3 / 'tag current image :prev, docker compose up -d --build'
findings.suggested_fix: After a successful `docker compose up -d` and health-check pass, run `docker image prune -f` to remove dangling layers. Optionally keep only `review-bot:prev` and `review-bot:latest` by name and prune others with a label filter.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the rollback precondition: before tagging `:prev`, verify `review-bot:latest` exists (or an image ID is captured from `docker compose images`); if absent, skip rollback-tag and document that rollback is unavailable for the first deploy, or pre-build the image as part of box bootstrap.
findings.finding: On a freshly provisioned box where the review-bot image has never been built (no `review-bot:latest` exists), `docker tag review-bot:latest review-bot:prev` will fail, leaving the rollback target undefined for the very first code-change deploy.
findings.location: Stability §3 / Box-adaptation §Adapted mechanism step 3
findings.suggested_fix: In `autodeploy.sh`, before tagging: `if docker image inspect review-bot:latest &>/dev/null; then docker tag review-bot:latest review-bot:prev; ROLLBACK_AVAILABLE=true; fi` — and only attempt rollback if `ROLLBACK_AVAILABLE=true`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `make config-check` is explicitly added to the Makefile AND added as a step in both `test.yml` AND `gerrit-verify.yaml` (the Gerrit Verified gate) — verifiable by grepping both files post-implementation.
findings.finding: `make config-check` does not exist in the Makefile, and `test.yml`/`gerrit-verify.yaml` have no `config-check` step — the plan's Defense-in-depth section and AC both assert this gate 'runs in `test.yml` (⇒ runs in `gerrit-verify` ⇒ the Verified gate)', but neither the Makefile target nor the CI wiring exists yet.
findings.location: Defense-in-depth section / AC line: 'CI config-gate: `make config-check` runs in `test.yml`/`gerrit-verify`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `config-check` target to the Makefile, then add a step `- name: Config validation (make config-check)\n  run: make config-check` to both `test.yml` (lint job) and `gerrit-verify.yaml` (build-and-test job).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify how the 'crash-looping container storm guard' is actually implemented for a compose-managed `review-bot` service: either document that Docker's `restart: unless-stopped` is the backstop (with any needed `--restart-max-attempts` config), or specify a systemd drop-in that wraps `docker compose restart review-bot` with actual StartLimit settings.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'systemd `StartLimitIntervalSec`/`StartLimitBurst` on the RUNNING bot service catches a crash-looping container', but the review-bot runs as a Docker container under `docker compose` with `restart: unless-stopped` — there is no systemd unit wrapping the bot container, so no systemd StartLimit settings can apply to it.
findings.location: Stability §4 (Failure handling) — 'Outer storm guard: systemd `StartLimitIntervalSec`/`StartLimitBurst` on the RUNNING bot service'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the autodeploy path manifest to use repo-root-relative paths matching `git diff --name-only` output: `infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot`, `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml`, `pyproject.toml` (not bare filenames).
findings.finding: The plan's source-set path manifest for review-bot change detection lists `Dockerfile.reviewbot` (bare filename), but the actual file lives at `infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot` — a `git diff --name-only` against the bare name will never match.
findings.location: What section — 'review-bot: src/rebar/**, Dockerfile.reviewbot, pyproject, docker-compose'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the manifest to: `src/rebar/** infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot infra/compose/docker-compose.yml pyproject.toml` for review-bot; `infra/gerrit/replication.config` for replication; `infra/gerrit/gerrit_to_platform.ini.template infra/gerrit/materialize-g2p-config.sh` for g2p.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify precisely how autodeploy applies a g2p config change: confirm it calls `materialize-g2p-config.sh` (requiring root + SSM instance role access), and that the systemd unit runs as root (or a role with SSM read permission), not as an unprivileged user.
findings.finding: The plan says 'g2p → re-materialise (autoReload / g2p copy)' and 'reuses the idempotent setup/materialise appliers', but `materialize-g2p-config.sh` fetches the GitHub PAT from SSM and renders the template — it is not a plain file copy. An autodeploy that only copies `gerrit_to_platform.ini.template` would leave the live `gerrit_to_platform.ini` (which holds the PAT) unchanged. If the autodeploy instead calls `materialize-g2p-config.sh` it needs SSM read access; the plan does not specify whether the autodeploy process runs with the instance role or as another principal.
findings.location: What section — 'replication/g2p → re-materialise (autoReload / g2p copy)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `Makefile` to the plan's explicit edit-set with the `config-check` target definition (commands: `git config -f {replication,project}.config --list`; `python configparser` on the g2p ini template; `docker compose config -q`; `bash -n` on `infra/**/*.sh`).
findings.finding: The plan's `make config-check` target does not exist in the Makefile, and the Makefile itself is never named as an edit target in the plan scope.
findings.location: Section 'Defense-in-depth: a CI gate that rejects malformed config'; AC line '`make config-check` runs in `test.yml`/`gerrit-verify`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a Makefile section to the plan's scope: 'Add `config-check` target to Makefile (new phony target, after `vendor-security-rules`) with the four parse-validation commands listed in the Defense-in-depth section.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `.github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml` to the plan's edit-set, explicitly noting that the same `make config-check` step added to `test.yml` must also be added to the `build-and-test` job in `gerrit-verify.yaml`.
findings.finding: `.github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml` is not named as an edit target, but the plan says `make config-check` wires into `gerrit-verify` — this file requires modification.
findings.location: Section 'Defense-in-depth'; AC '`make config-check` runs in `test.yml`/`gerrit-verify` and FAILS on a deliberately-malformed config'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `infra/scripts/observability.sh` to the plan's edit-set with a description of the new section to add (grep `AUTODEPLOY_ERROR` from the autodeploy service unit's journald entries, publish `rebar/host:deploy_errors` delta, following the same offset-file pattern as `voter_errors`).
findings.finding: `infra/scripts/observability.sh` is an unenumerated consumer: the plan's `rebar/host:deploy_errors` metric (mirroring `voter_errors`) requires observability.sh to be modified to grep `AUTODEPLOY_ERROR` from journald and publish the delta, but observability.sh is never named as an edit target.
findings.location: Section 'Observability + back-out': '`rebar/host:deploy_errors` (host-probe grep of an `AUTODEPLOY_ERROR` marker, mirroring voter_errors)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name and scope an installer script (e.g. `infra/scripts/install-autodeploy.sh`) in the plan's edit-set, following the pattern of `install-observability.sh`, that writes the `rebar-autodeploy.timer` and `rebar-autodeploy.service` unit files to `/etc/systemd/system/` and runs `systemctl daemon-reload && systemctl enable --now rebar-autodeploy.timer`.
findings.finding: The plan names an `install-autodeploy.sh` (or equivalent) script to install the systemd timer/service units on the box, but no such installer script is named in the plan's Scope or edit-set, and there is no existing pattern file to reference for it.
findings.location: Section 'Box-adaptation of the deploy mechanism'; Trigger section: '`rebar-autodeploy.timer` (2-min) → `.service` oneshot'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: 'infra/scripts/install-autodeploy.sh — new installer (mirrors install-observability.sh pattern): writes unit files + daemon-reload + enable.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that the autodeploy 'replication apply' step explicitly copies the rsynced `infra/gerrit/replication.config` to `/var/gerrit/site/etc/replication.config` (and chowns it to gerrit uid) BEFORE relying on `autoReload = true` to pick up the change — rsync alone does not update the live site file that the replication plugin watches.
findings.finding: The plan's replication.config apply step relies on `autoReload = true` to pick up changes, but `autoReload` fires only when the LIVE GERRIT SITE file (`/var/gerrit/site/etc/replication.config`) changes — rsync only updates the repo-root source copy at `/opt/rebar/infra/gerrit/replication.config`. The autodeploy must also explicitly copy the rsynced file to the live site path (as `setup-replication.sh` does with its `cp $SRC_CONFIG $DST_CONFIG` step) before `autoReload` can fire. An implementer following the plan as written could rsync + trust autoReload and the live Gerrit plugin would never see the change.
findings.location: What section — 'Apply only what changed: replication/g2p → re-materialise (autoReload / g2p copy)'; Box-adaptation section — step 2 rsync
findings.suggested_fix: In the autodeploy replication apply block, after rsync completes, run: `cp /opt/rebar/infra/gerrit/replication.config /var/gerrit/site/etc/replication.config && chown 1000:1000 /var/gerrit/site/etc/replication.config` (mirroring setup-replication.sh lines 92-94). autoReload then picks up the file change. The deploy key re-materialization (SSM call) in setup-replication.sh is NOT needed here unless the key itself changed — the autodeploy should skip that step on a config-only change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Make explicit in the plan that the g2p apply step re-invokes `materialize-g2p-config.sh` (the SSM PAT fetch + template render + file write) rather than a plain file copy from /opt/rebar — the PAT is secret and the template placeholder must be substituted at apply time, not baked into the rsynced source.
findings.finding: The g2p re-materialisation step requires running `materialize-g2p-config.sh` (which fetches the GitHub PAT from SSM, renders the template with PAT substitution, and copies replication.config into the g2p config dir), but the plan does not explicitly state that the autodeploy invokes this script. The plan lists 'g2p ini + materialize script' in the change-detect path manifest and says 're-materialise (... g2p copy)' as the apply action, but never names the mechanism: the PAT is a secret that cannot be in the rsynced source, so a plain file copy from /opt/rebar is insufficient — `materialize-g2p-config.sh` (with its SSM call) is the required apply step.
findings.location: What section — 'Apply only what changed: replication/g2p → re-materialise (autoReload / g2p copy)'; Box-adaptation section — step 2 rsync
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Apply only what changed' section, replace 'g2p → re-materialise (... g2p copy)' with 'g2p → re-run `infra/gerrit/materialize-g2p-config.sh`' (the script already handles the SSM PAT fetch, template render, 0600 output, and replication.config copy into the g2p config dir — it is idempotent and safe to re-run).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit prior-art note evaluating Watchtower (and optionally CodeDeploy agent-pull) for the deploy-loop pattern, and document why the custom bash loop was chosen over them (e.g. source-rebuild model, no image registry, git-based change-detection requirement).
findings.finding: Watchtower — the canonical Docker-native tool for auto-updating running compose-stack services — is the closest direct prior art for this plan's core loop (poll for changes, selectively rebuild/restart a single service, roll back on failure) and is neither mentioned nor explicitly dismissed. The plan cites Flux/Argo CD for backoff policy but not for the deploy-loop pattern itself, which is exactly what Watchtower implements. The security-rationale (source rebuild from git, no image-registry push model, single-tenant box) may well justify custom over Watchtower, but that dismissal is absent.
findings.location: ## What — 'Loop' section; Stability §1–4; ## Trigger (RESOLVED)
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Trigger (RESOLVED)' or a new 'Alternatives considered' subsection, add: 'Watchtower was evaluated and rejected because it operates on pre-built/published images (registry poll), not source rebuilds — our loop must rebuild from source to pick up Dockerfile/dependency changes, making a custom loop necessary despite the maintenance cost. CodeDeploy agent-pull was also considered but adds IAM/agent complexity without the security advantage over the existing on-box timer pattern.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact `git clone` invocation (flags included) for MIRROR_DIR setup in the plan, and verify that `rev-parse origin/main` and `git diff --name-only deployed-sha..origin/main` resolve correctly in that clone type (a regular `git clone` with remote `origin` is the correct choice for these ref forms; `--mirror` is NOT).
findings.finding: Unverified capability assertion: the plan's change-detection relies on `git diff --name-only deployed-sha..origin/main` and `rev-parse origin/main` running inside a self-maintained clone at `MIRROR_DIR=/var/lib/rebar/mirror`, but the plan never specifies the clone type (`git clone`, `git clone --bare`, or `git clone --mirror`). These three modes name remote-tracking refs differently: a regular clone exposes `origin/main`; a `--bare` or `--mirror` clone does NOT have a remote named `origin` and uses `refs/heads/main` or `FETCH_HEAD` directly. If the wrong clone type is used, `origin/main` will fail to resolve, silently breaking change-detection.
findings.location: ## What — 'Loop' section; ## Box-adaptation — 'Adapted mechanism' step 1
findings.suggested_fix: Use a regular `git clone https://github.com/navapbc/rebar.git /var/lib/rebar/mirror` (no `--mirror`/`--bare` flags) and update via `git fetch origin`. This makes `origin/main` resolve correctly for all the git operations cited. Document this in the 'Adapted mechanism' step 1.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an `infra/scripts/install-autodeploy.sh` script (mirroring `install-observability.sh`) that installs `autodeploy.sh` to its target path, writes the systemd `.service` and `.timer` unit files, initialises `MIRROR_DIR` if absent, and calls `systemctl daemon-reload && systemctl enable --now rebar-autodeploy.timer` — and wire this script into `user_data.sh` or the setup runbook so the units are reproducibly installed on a fresh box.
findings.finding: The new systemd units (rebar-autodeploy.timer + rebar-autodeploy.service) and the autodeploy.sh script are described in the plan but no install script (analogous to the existing install-observability.sh) or IaC mechanism is specified to place and enable them on the box. Without this, the units are not reproducible as-code: a box rebuild or a fresh apply leaves no automated path to re-install them.
findings.location: Plan section 'Box-adaptation of the deploy mechanism' / 'systemd unit fix'; existing pattern: infra/scripts/install-observability.sh
findings.suggested_fix: Create infra/scripts/install-autodeploy.sh following the same pattern as install-observability.sh: copy autodeploy.sh to /usr/local/bin/rebar-autodeploy.sh, write the .service and .timer unit files to /etc/systemd/system/, run `git clone --mirror <public-mirror-url> $MIRROR_DIR` if the mirror dir is absent, then `systemctl daemon-reload && systemctl enable --now rebar-autodeploy.timer`. Reference this script in user_data.sh (or the S2 setup runbook) so it runs on any fresh box.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `infra/terraform/monitoring_autodeploy.tf` declaring an `aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm` for `rebar/host:deploy_errors` (matching the namespace/metric-name/dimension contract the host probe publishes) and wired to the existing `aws_sns_topic.alerts`, following the pattern of `monitoring_s4b.tf`.
findings.finding: The plan's `deploy_errors` CloudWatch metric and alarm are described in prose ('rebar/host:deploy_errors … mirroring voter_errors … a CloudWatch alarm w/ SNS action') but no corresponding `monitoring_autodeploy.tf` (or equivalent IaC) is specified. Every existing alarm in the repo is managed as a named .tf file (monitoring.tf, monitoring_s4b.tf, monitoring_s5.tf, etc.); without an IaC declaration the alarm has no reproducible as-code definition and no clear teardown path.
findings.location: Plan section 'Observability + back-out'; existing pattern: infra/terraform/monitoring_s4b.tf, monitoring_s5.tf
findings.suggested_fix: Create infra/terraform/monitoring_autodeploy.tf with an aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm resource for metric_name = "deploy_errors", namespace = "rebar/host", statistic = "Sum", period = 300, evaluation_periods = 1, threshold = 0, comparison_operator = "GreaterThanThreshold", treat_missing_data = "notBreaching", alarm_actions = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn], mirroring monitoring_s4b.tf's voter_errors alarm structure.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the post-deploy health check will use the existing `/health` liveness probe or a new/extended readiness check that verifies reconciler/worker task liveness; if the existing probe is used, document the known gap (liveness ≠ review-readiness) and adjust the AC wording accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the 30s post-deploy health check 'confirms the reconciler is live' and that the bot 'can actually review, not just process-up', but the existing `GET /health` endpoint in `src/rebar/review_bot/app.py` is a pure liveness probe that returns `{"status": "ok"}` as soon as FastAPI starts — it does NOT verify the reconciler task or the review worker are running. There is no existing /readiness or deeper check endpoint.
findings.location: Stability §3 ('post-deploy health check 30s timeout — bot can actually review, not just process-up') + AC line 4 (self-heal) + src/rebar/review_bot/app.py line 113–116
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) add a `/readiness` endpoint that inspects `app.state.tasks` for live (non-cancelled, non-done) task objects and returns 503 if any are dead, or (b) restate the health check as a liveness probe in the plan and narrow the AC to 'bot process is up and accepting requests' rather than 'bot can actually review'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a specified initialization procedure for `MIRROR_DIR`: either a bootstrap block in `autodeploy.sh` itself (`[ -d "$MIRROR_DIR/.git" ] || git clone --mirror <PUBLIC_MIRROR_URL> "$MIRROR_DIR"`) or a named install script analogous to `install-observability.sh`, with the public GitHub mirror URL stated.
findings.finding: The adapted mechanism depends on `MIRROR_DIR=/var/lib/rebar/mirror` already existing as a git clone (all git operations — fetch, rev-parse, diff — run there), but the plan never specifies how this clone is initialized: what URL, whether authentication is needed, which bootstrap script creates it, or what the first-run behavior is when the directory is absent.
findings.location: Box-adaptation section ('autodeploy maintains its OWN git mirror clone at MIRROR_DIR=/var/lib/rebar/mirror') + Additional AC ('autodeploy syncs source into the copy-based /opt/rebar via a self-maintained mirror clone')
findings.suggested_fix: In `autodeploy.sh`, add an early guard: `[ -d "$MIRROR_DIR/.git" ] || git clone --mirror https://github.com/navapbc/rebar.git "$MIRROR_DIR"` (public URL, no auth needed per the plan's own note). This makes first-run self-bootstrapping and satisfies the AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify and enforce that `git clone` / `git fetch` for the mirror uses `https://` (not `git://`), and document the concrete clone URL in the plan or autodeploy.sh spec so the protocol is unambiguous and auditable.
findings.finding: The plan never specifies that the mirror clone URL uses HTTPS; if git:// (unauthenticated plaintext) is used, a MITM on the outbound path can inject arbitrary content into the fetched git objects, which get rsync'd into the build context and executed by the deploy script.
findings.location: Box-adaptation section — 'Adapted mechanism', step 1: 'autodeploy maintains its OWN git mirror clone at MIRROR_DIR=/var/lib/rebar/mirror (a clone of the PUBLIC GitHub mirror, read-only)'
findings.suggested_fix: In autodeploy.sh, initialise the mirror with `git clone --mirror https://github.com/<org>/<repo>.git $MIRROR_DIR` and fetch with `git -C $MIRROR_DIR fetch --prune origin`; add a guard that aborts if the remote URL in the mirror is not `https://` (e.g. `git -C $MIRROR_DIR remote get-url origin | grep -q '^https://' || { echo 'AUTODEPLOY_ERROR: mirror remote is not HTTPS'; exit 1; }`).

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
